summary: Joel announces major changes to Stack Exchange, pivoting from a paid white-label hosted product to a free, community-driven platform. The original Stack Exchange model let anyone create a Q&A site, but didn't produce enough high-quality sites. The new model makes Stack Exchange free, introduces a democratic community process for creating new sites, and places all content under Creative Commons licensing rather than private ownership.
key_points: Stack Overflow's ambitions grew from programming Q&A to wanting to cover every topic
The paid white-label Stack Exchange model failed to produce the expected flood of high-quality community sites
The pivot to Stack Exchange 2.0 makes the platform free, community-governed, and publicly licensed under Creative Commons
Existing Stack Exchange site owners are assured their sites won't be disrupted during the transition
money_quotes: Like the small-town mayor who suddenly finds herself running an entire state, our ambitions for Stack Overflow keep growing.
Programming questions? We asked. Why just programming questions? Why not every question under the sun?
We thought that other people would create awesome sites on every imaginable topic. Some people did (yay!), but it wasn't the flood of high quality sites we were hoping for.
Stack Exchange will now be free.
The content of these new, community-created Stack Exchange sites will be publically owned under a Creative Commons license, instead of being owned by individuals or businesses.
key_insight: When a paid platform model fails to generate sufficient community-driven content, making it free and community-governed can be the necessary pivot to achieve scale and quality.
